Arabs here, I think, look at the West and they go, well, they don't get us.

But the Axis of Evil trio had to censor themselves when it came to other subjects that are taboo in the Middle East.
In some countries, it was completely off-limits to joke about Islam or about dictatorial host governments.
Egypt, we had to hold back; Dubai, we hold back.
What happens is these government officials come up and they watch the shows.
And if somebody insults somebody, then they just shut down the show.
Can you see the government officials in the theater?

Their objective in going to Congress is not to save the industry.
Their objective in going to Congress is to get money from Congress.
And if you read through these documents, they are - especially the General Motors document, it is full of really shameless Americanizing, patriotic propaganda.
It's geared to getting the money, and that's their objective.
And I believe they will come away from Washington with money.
So, in terms of their objective, they will succeed in that narrow objective.
However, if you ask me: Will the shareholders, or will anybody who has their debt, ever see a return on that capital?
That, I'm a lot more skeptical about.
Evan Newmark is a columnist for the Wall Street Journal.
His column in today's paper is titled ""Why GM is Doomed."" Evan, thank you.

First, with the election only weeks away, more than 5 million Americans are barred from voting because they're convicted felons.
And even in those places where felons can legally vote, they may not know how to claim the right.
There are different laws.
As Day to Day's Alex Cohen reports, even election officials are confused.
Thirty-nine-year-old Alex Friedmann of Memphis has never voted for president.
In 1987, I committed the crimes of armed robbery and assault, attempt to commit murder.
I entered the prison system when I was 18.
Friedmann was released in 1999 and he finished parole in 2005.
Shortly after that, the state of Tennessee passed a complicated law that requires ex-offenders like Friedmann to pay off all restitution fees before registering to vote.
In order to regain my voting rights in Tennessee, under the law, I had to pay $900.
After months of legal wrangling, Friedmann was finally able to register last Friday.
We have a history-setting presidential election coming up in the United States.
It's good to be able to participate in that.
But not everyone believes former felons should be able to participate.
If you are not willing to follow the laws, then you can't claim a right to make the laws for everyone else.
Roger Clegg is president of the Center for Equal Opportunity, a conservative think tank.
That's what you do when you vote.
You're either making the law directly, if you're voting on a referendum or something like that, or you're making it indirectly by choosing lawmakers.
Virginia, where Clegg lives, and Kentucky have the most stringent laws.
Former felons are permanently barred from voting unless the governor personally restores their rights.
These laws vary from state to state.
Rachel Bloom of the ACLU notes that 20 states require a prisoner to complete both parole and probation before voting.
And then there's the most lenient end of the spectrum.
Maine and Vermont are the two states where people can vote while in prison.
Bloom says there are also states where laws are murky, like Alabama.
